coredata.dc:title: Red emissive N–S co-doped carbon dots for live imaging of tumor spheroid in the microfluidic device
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Science: Advanced Materials and Devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ermis, Esra
Bagheri, Zeinab
Behroodi, Ebrahim
Latifi, Hamid
Rahimifard, Mahban
Ajorlou, Elham
coredata.dc:description: Here, we present a one-pot, water-based synthesis of red emissive biocompatible carbon dots (R-CDs) for live bioimaging of U87 microglia tumor spheroids produced in a microwell-based microfluidic device. R-CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method, using thiourea and para-phenylenediamine as the starting precursors. The mold of the microfluidic platform was produced by a lab-made high-resolution optical 3D printer and CNC micro-milling. The optical and physical properties of synthesized R-CDs were validated by different analytical methods such as fluorescent and absorption spectroscopy, FT-IR, DLS, and TEM. The prepared R-CDs emit red light with a maximum fluorescent wavelength of 584 nm. The prepared R-CD has low cytotoxicity and has been successfully applied for staining the glioblastoma spheroid without any fixation step. Therefore, it is a promising fluorescent probe in live 3D cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spheroid
Red carbon dots
Microwell microfluidic chip
Live-cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and fabrication of the microfluidic chip
Evaluation of the toxicity of R-CDs toward spheroids
Spheroid formation and characterization of spheroids
Evaluation of the R-CDs on spheroid formation
Fluorescent imaging of spheroids using R-CDs as the fluorescent dye

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The microwell chip's manufacturing steps: (a) 3D designing mold (b) Numerical fluid flow simulation (c) and (d) Scanning electron micrographs of the side view of the mold before and after coating with PMMA. (e) The exploded view of the master mold, which consists of one printed part and two micro CNC milling parts. (f) PDMS casting for both microwell and lid layers. (g) The view of the microwell layer under a microscope. (h) The microwell and cover PDMS layers bonded together using the plasma bonding procedure to fabricate the microfluidic device.
(a) UV−vis absorption and emission spectra excited at 456 nm, (b) emission of CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (c) TEM image of the prepared R-CDs, (d) FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs.

Characterization of the spheroid formation on-chip: (a–e) Spheroid formation of U87 glioma cancer cells in microwells: (a) cell loading to microfluidic chips. (b) The view of U87 cell in microwells under a microscope. (c) The view of U87 cells in microwells under the microscope after washing with cell medium. (d) After cell loading. (e) Spheroid formation of U87 glioma cancer cells in microwells.

Result of the viability of U87 cells by MTT assay after 24 h treatment with different concentrations of R-CDs. Obtained results are shown as mean ± standard error of three different tests (n = 3). ∗ indicates significant differences from the control at p < 0.01.
The formation of a spheroid in the absence (a) and presence (b) of CD after 3 h, 24 h, 48 h. (c) spheroid size changed with treatment time.

The result of a viability investigation using AO/PI staining after two days of spheroid formation. The green color for the merged image demonstrates the high percentage of live cells versus dead cells.
Spheroid staining with R-CDs. In (a), the spheroid formation was performed in the presence of R-CDs, but in the (b), treatment with R-CDs was conducted after spheroid formation.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of Vietnam National University, Hanoi.

head.ce:title: Red emissive N–S co-doped carbon dots for live imaging of tumor spheroid in the microfluidic device

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laser and Plasma Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, 1983963113, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laser and Plasma Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, 1983963113, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Toxicology and Disease Group, Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Toxicology and Disease Group, Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we present a one-pot, water-based synthesis of red emissive biocompatible carbon dots (R-CDs) for live bioimaging of U87 microglia tumor spheroids produced in a microwell-based microfluidic device. R-CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method, using thiourea and para-phenylenediamine as the starting precursors. The mold of the microfluidic platform was produced by a lab-made high-resolution optical 3D printer and CNC micro-milling. The optical and physical properties of synthesized R-CDs were validated by different analytical methods such as fluorescent and absorption spectroscopy, FT-IR, DLS, and TEM. The prepared R-CDs emit red light with a maximum fluorescent wavelength of 584 nm. The prepared R-CD has low cytotoxicity and has been successfully applied for staining the glioblastoma spheroid without any fixation step. Therefore, it is a promising fluorescent probe in live 3D cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Live-cell fluorescence techniques have now been developed for tracking cell behavior. For this purpose, different fluorescent dyes that do not cause cell death have been developed. Increasing the quantum yield (QY), enhancing the photo and chemical stability, and adjusting the emission wavelength are significant challenges to improve these dyes and increase their performance in live-cell fluorescent imaging. Moreover, considerable investigations have been conducted to develop innovative ways to culture cells in 3D and miniaturize cultures for high-throughput experiments. However, most fluorescent probes have been designed for 2D cultures; therefore, it is necessary to evaluate and optimize them in 3D cell culture [1].

Carbon dots (CDs), recently introduced as fluorescent dyes for bioimaging applications, have exciting features such as up-conversion property, brightness, photostability, and high biocompatibility [2–5]. It has been reported in many synthetic studies that it is relatively easy to synthesize the green and blue CDs with high QY. But there are many limitations to using blue and green CDs in bioimaging, including low penetration depth for short-wavelength photons, strong tissue absorption in these spectra, photodamage potential, and intense blue autofluorescence of biological tissues [6–8]. Therefore, the synthesis of CDs with emission in the red region has attracted increasing attention due to their superior properties such as deep tissue penetration, low level of interference from autofluorescence of biomolecules, and minimal photodamage by the red light [8–10]. However, there is a significant difficulty in preparing red-emitting CDs (R-CDs) with high QY; This is mainly because a larger domain of sp2 π-conjugated carbon is required for red emission. Finally, the low QY and the low water solubility of many reported R-CDs limited the development of the CDs-based bioimaging application [6,7,10].

Using three-dimensional(3D) cell culture models allows a better simulation of the tumor microenvironment conditions such as hypoxia and nutrient gradients, and, therefore, they are a suitable cell model for investigating the effectiveness of drugs and cytotoxicity studies [11–13]. Among the 3D cell culture models, a multicellular tumor spheroid is a self-organized group of cells with gradients in oxygen, nutrients, metabolites, and catabolites that naturally mimic the structure and function of the solid tumor tissue [14]. Various methods have been used for spheroid formation, including spinner flask [15,16], hanging-drop technique [17,18], micropatterning [19–21] and microfluidic devices [22–24]. Microfluidics provides a powerful approach for producing the spheroid due to its unique properties, such as handling the liquid in small volume, requiring low sample and reagents and cost-effective manufacturing [1,25]. Besides, microfluidics can better control heat and material transfer in spatial and temporal resolution, allowing well-defined microenvironment conditions for studying cell physiology [26–28]. Eventually, microfluidic 3D cell culture systems upgrade culture models' complexity to a level similar to in vivo models [29,30]. Among the different microfluidic fabrication methods, 3D printing is a promising approach due to its simplicity and flexibility in producing different complex 3D structures.

Several studies have used microfluidics to prepare the 3D cell culture and to evaluate the cytotoxicity of nanomaterials on these cell models inside the chip. For instance, J. Zhuang et al. generated a bulk of spheroids and evaluated mesoporous silica nanoparticles' cellular internalization in MCF-7 spheroids under shear force inside the microfluidic device [28]. On the other hand, Lee et al. developed a hydrogel microwell array to produce uniform sized spheroids. Moreover, to perform anticancer therapeutics, J. Lee et al. developed graphene hybrid nanomaterial (Au@GO) for photothermal therapy and evaluated the effectiveness of these nanomaterials in a series of tumor spheroid co-culture systems [31].

In this study, we applied a microfluidic platform to facilitate the extensive screening of multicellular spheroids to test R-CDs' effects on human glioblastoma (GBM) cells. We synthesized R-CDs through the hydrothermal synthesis method from para-phenylenediamine (p-PDA) and thiourea. The synthesized R-CDs were also characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and spectrofluorometry methods. The toxicity of R-CDs was evaluated in 2D cell culture and 3D cell culture. For cytotoxicity assay of R-CDs in 3D cell culture, we cultured U87 tumor cells as 3D spheroids in the microwell-based microfluidic chip. After confirming the advanced optical properties of our R-CDs and evaluating their cytotoxicity, the R-CDs were finally applied to bio-imaging a spheroid as a fluorescent probe. Our research also proved the potency of the microfluidic device in generating a 3D cell model for high-throughput nanomaterial biocompatibility assays.

P-phenylenediamine (PPD, 99.9%), thiourea, rhodamine 6G (Rh6G), and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide (MTT powder) were purchased from the Sigma Aldrich company. Ethanol and isopropanol were obtained from Mojallali (Tehran, Iran). PDMS, Sylgard 184 Elastomer Kit, was purchased from the Dow corning company (USA). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), Fetal bovine serum (FBS), were purchased from the Gibco Company (USA). Penicillin/streptomycin, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), trypsin/EDTA, and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were obtained from the Merck Company (Germany). Acridine Orange(OA) and Propidium iodide (PI) were bought from the Sigma Aldrich company.

In this study, the R-CDs were produced by the hydrothermal approach. First, p-phenylenediamine (0.1 g) and thiourea (0.1 g) were dissolved in 20 mL of water and the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Then, the autoclave was heated for 12 h at 200 °C. In the next step, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Then, 1 mL of the obtained solution was added to the 9 mL of water and the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was collected and filtered by a syringe filter (0.22 μm). The obtained R-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C and was stable for at least four weeks.

A JEOL-2010 electron microscope was applied to capture transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. A Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer was utilized to record the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. An F-7000 Hitachi fluorescence spectrofluorometer was used to calculate the photoluminescence spectra. For realizing the optical features of the R-CD solution, ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (LAMBDA 950, PerkinElmer, USA) was used [32,33]. A rhodamine 6G solution in ethanol was used to determine the QY of prepared R-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of the microfluidic chip
ce:section.ce:para: For designing different parts of the master mold, SolıdWorks software was used. The microfluidic device contains 57 microwells (Fig. 1(a)), laid down in a chamber with one inlet and one outlet. The height of the chamber and the depth of microwells, 200 μm and 300 μm, respectively, were evaluated. The conical micro-wells have an angle of 25° in the axial direction and the top plane with the regular hexagon shape has a diameter of 500 μm.

A series of finite element calculations (FEM) was conducted by the COMSOL multiphysics package to explore the velocity distribution inside the microfluidic device. The governing equations for Newtonian fluid simulation inside a chip are the continuity equation (Eq. (1)) and the three-dimensional (3D) Navier–Stokes equation (Eq. (2)).
(1)
∇ ⋅ V = 0
(2)
ρ
( ∂ V ∂ t + V ⋅ ∇ V )
= − ∇ p + μ ∇ 2 V

In the above equations, V, ρ, μ and p represent velocity vector, density, viscosity, and pressure, respectively. Eq. (2) is simplified by ignoring the effect of body forces. Boundary conditions were non-slip wall conditions, incompressible flow and steady-state. The flow rate at the outlet and inlet was specified as zero and 50 μL/min, respectively. In addition, a tetrahedral grid was employed to discretize the computational domain (Fig. 1(b)).

Because of the structural complexity of conical microwells, we used the lab-made PμSL 3D printer to fabricate the microwell's mold [34,35]. Then, a scanning electron microscope (FEI-Quanta 200) was used to evaluate the printed parts' size, geometry and quality. Before the SEM imaging, samples were secured to the holder. Then, they were covered with a thin gold film for 120 s (Fig. 1(c & d)). Due to the approximately six μm transverse resolution of the PμSL printer, the printed object has a maximum size of 7.7 mm × 4.8 mm, which is too small to produce a chip. For this reason, the whole master mold cannot be printed using the PμSL printer. Therefore, CNC micro-milling was used to produce channels and the central part of the chip. Then, the various parts of the final master mold are assembled and used for PDMS casting (Fig. 1(e)).

The U87 human glioblastoma cell line obtained from the medical university of Tehran (Iran) was cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM high-glucose) with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin–streptomycin. The U87 cells were incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 [36]. Then 0.25% Trypsin–EDTA was used to subculture the cells. Later analyses were carried out using cells between passages 3 and 6 at 80% confluence.

The sterilization of the microfluidic device was performed with high-power UV light for 20 min. The device was kept in a vacuum chamber for 40 min right before use to prevent bubble formation. In the next step, to prevent cells from adhering, BSA 3% (w/v) was infused through the microchannel and incubated for 1 h. Later, to remove uncoated BSA, the device was washed three times with PBS. A sanitized microfluidic chip was filled through the inlet with 100 μl of media. Then, cells (1 × 106 cells/ml) were carefully filled via the cell input port using a micropipette. After 15 min, the inlet was filled with the fresh medium and the waste medium with excess cells was removed from the microfluidic device. Following the seeding of the cells, chips were incubated to allow spheroid formation. The medium was changed once a day to provide adequate nutrients for spheroid formation and cell proliferation.

For examining the toxicity effects of R-CDs on U87 cells, Cell proliferation and their viability were investigated using the MTT assay. Shortly, R-CD-treated cells were exposed to 50 μl MTT reagent (0.5 mg/ml) at 37 °C for 3 h. Later, 150 μl DMSO was added to each sample to dissolve the formazan crystal that emerged after MTT was reduced in living cells. After a period of 30 min, a microplate reader (Biotech, Winooski, VT) was used to measure the absorbance rate at 570 and 630 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the toxicity of R-CDs toward spheroids
ce:section.ce:para: We evaluated the toxicity effect of R-CDs toward 3D cell culture in two ways inside the microfluidic device, meanwhile studying the formation of the spheroid in the presence of R-CDs and treating the formed spheroids with the R-CDs solution. For performing this part of the study, three groups of the sanitized chip were used. For the first and second groups, the culture medium containing 1 × 106 cells/ml was carefully injected into the chip and, after 15 min, unattached cells were removed by injecting 100 μl culture medium. Following the seeding of the cells, the chips were incubated to allow spheroid formation. After 24 h, the medium of the first and second groups was changed with fresh medium and fresh medium containing 50 μg/mL of R-CDs, respectively. The initial cell seeding medium contained 50 μg/mL of R-CDs for the third group and the medium was changed after 24 h. For all three groups, after 48 h, the spheroids were washed with PBS. Following the incubation with an AO/PI solution for 10 min in the dark, the spheroids were washed with PBS. Next, imaging was carried out using a fluorescent microscope (Nikon, Japan) in the red and green channels. Moreover, to examine the coloring potential of R-CDs, the fluorescent images (red channel) were recorded for spheroids of groups 2 and 3.

At least three independent tests were conducted for every experiment and the obtained results were presented as a mean ± SEM. To assess the statistical differences, a one-way ANOVA was performed. The p-value of 0.01 was considered to be significant.

Microwell chips and arrays are commonly utilized as functional techniques to produce tumor spheroids. It has been reported that flat-bottomed microwells are less efficient in producing spheroids than conical or concave microwells. The microwell bottom with its conical shape provides more effective cell aggregation [34,37]. So far, several methods have been used to produce non-flat-bottomed microwell chips [38,39]. For instance, to construct cell-loss-free (CLF) microwell arrays, Lee et al. used the simple operation of laser carving of a poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) master mold in their study [39]. Besides, Jarvinen et al. used single-step lithography techniques to manufacture a concave microwell chip [40].

The 3D designing mold view of the chip is depicted in Fig. 1(a). The numerical results of fluid flow simulation for a chip with 300 μm-deep microwells are shown in Fig. 1(b). As can be inferred from the figure, flow is uniformly distributed for 57 microwells. Besides, simulations show a higher flow rate in the upper layer than in the microwell, contributing to cell trapping and spheroid formation. Advanced laboratory equipment and competent personnel are required to manufacture high-resolution microfluidic components using traditional technologies. Hence, simplification as much as possible is necessary to make a microfluidic chip for use in biological laboratories. Fig. 1(c)–(h) demonstrates all the stages (from producing the master mold to casting with PDMS) of manufacturing the microfluidic chips. Fig. 1(e) presents the various parts of a master mold that includes a 3D printing template and two CNC micro-milling parts that have been exploded. The channels and main chamber are produced in CNC part 1 and part 2. For the production of chips, the plasma bonding technique was used to bond the cover and microwell PDMS layers. (Fig. 1(f)).

Fig. 1(c & d) shows the SEM images of the printed molds of the microwell. In Fig. 1(c), the mold's side view with microwell heights (300 μm) has been shown. Different resin components of the printed part prevent the polymerization of the PDMS at the contact surface of the mold, which causes the PDMS to attach to the mold surface after baking. A PMMA/chloroform solution was applied to coat the printing's surface for solving this problem. The coating characteristics on the surface of the mold are shown in the images obtained with a scanning electron microscope (Fig. 1(d)). Therefore, after utilizing the PMMA, a uniform layer is formed on the surface of the mold. Fig. 1(g) shows the microwell layer under the microscope. Fig. 1(h) shows the final PDMS microfluidic chip. The chip is 30
×
15
×
5 mm3 in size and consists of two PDMS parts connected using a plasma bonding process. The lower part contains microwells, channels, and chambers. The top part is the chip cover, and the inlet and outlet holes are punched therein.

Fig. 2 illustrates the characteristic analysis of the synthesized R-CDs. The UV–Vis and fluorescent spectroscopy results are presented in Fig. 2(a & b), which shows maximum absorption and emission wavelengths at 456 and 584 nm, respectively. The full-width half-maximum (FWHM) below 100 nm confirms a homogeneity in the nanoparticles' diameter distribution. Rhodamine 6G was used as a standard fluorescent reagent to evaluate the fluorescence efficiency of materials with long-wavelength emissions. The QY of the synthesized R-CDs was obtained at 13%. Fig. 1(b) shows that the synthesized CDs' emission wavelength is independent of the excitation wavelength. The morphology of the R-CDs was further studied by transmission electron microscopy. The TEM image showed a relatively uniform diameter distribution and revealed an average lateral size of 4.2 nm for the R-CDs Fig. 2(c) The FT-IR spectra of R-CDs, demonstrated in Fig. 2(d), have the following components namely O–H (3412 cm−1), N–H (3328 cm−1), C–C/C–O/C–S (1234 cm−1), and NCS (2012 cm−1). Hydrophilic groups such as N–H, O–H, and C–O on the R-CDs surfaces ensure a good solubility in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spheroid formation and characterization of spheroids
ce:section.ce:para: The developed microfluidic device comprises conical microwells that provide a suitable geometrical condition for 3D tumor spheroid formation. U87 cells can be infused into the chip by pipetting, reflecting the simplicity of using this microfluidic platform (Fig. 3 (a)). The solution containing one million cells/ml was injected with pipetting using a sampler. After 10 min, a 100 μl cell culture medium was infused into the chip to remove the un-seeded cells. Fig. 3(b) shows the chip after cell injection. Fig. 3(c) depicts the wells after washing with the cell culture medium. The design of this chip is such that all the microwells are uniformly filled with cells (Fig. 3(d)), and, therefore, the spheroids that are formed in different wells have the same size (Fig. 3(e)). In addition, the conical shape of microwells leads to the formation of a single spheroid in each microwell.
Fig. 4 shows the formation of spheroids in the microwells after 1 h, 3 h, 12 h, 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h. The obtained results indicate an increment of the spheroid compactness for three days.

To measure the effect of different concentrations of R-CDs on U87 cells, cells were treated with different concentrations of R-CDs (0–160 mg/ml) and the growth inhibitory effect of R-CDs was evaluated using the MTT assay. Fig. 5 shows the viability (percent) of the U87 cells in the different concentrations of R-CDs. As it's clear, all the studied concentrations of the R-CDs did not show a significant decrease in mitochondrial activity. The addition of R-CDs at the concentrations in 120 and 160 μg/mL to U87 cells did not reduce the cell viability by more than 10%, confirming that R-CDs have low cytotoxicity and could be utilized in bioimaging and other biomedical applications at relatively high concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the R-CDs on spheroid formation
ce:section.ce:para: The previous section showed that the synthesized carbon dot has good biocompatibility in the 2D cell model. But as we want to use this dye for spheroid staining, the spheroid formation was also investigated in the presence of R-CDs. Fig. 6 shows the formation of the spheroid in the absence and presence of prepared R-CDs. R-CDs seemed to slow down the formation of spheroids, but, eventually, spheroids were formed (Fig. 6(a & b)). (Fig. 6(c) shows the change in the average diameter for all spheroids in the microwells after 3 h, 24 h, and 48 h. The trend of changes in the formation of spheroids in the presence and absence of R-CDs is the same, but up to 48 h after cell seeding, the rate of formation of spheroids without the presence of a carbon dot is not much higher than when the carbon dot is present in the environment.

Fig. 7 shows spheroids cultured in the microwell, without R-CDs, with R-CDs, and treated with R-CDs after spheroid formation. In addition, Fig. 7 shows the viability assay results using AO/PI staining after two days of spheroid formation. The green color of the merged image shows a high percentage of living cells compared to dead cells. According to the image analysis, it was found that the viability of the proliferating layer is more than 90%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent imaging of spheroids using R-CDs as the fluorescent dye
ce:section.ce:para: According to our previous results, which demonstrated that the synthesized R-CDs do not have much cytotoxicity and do not prevent spheroid formation, the prepared R-CDs were used for imaging of the spheroid. Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence imaging of spheroids treated with the synthesized R-CDs. Imaging was carried out for spheroids that formed in the presence of R-CDs (Fig. 8(a)) and for spheroids that were treated with R-CDs after their formation (Fig. 8(b)). A Red-fluorescence signal was observed in both cases but is stronger for Fig. 8(a). Due to live-cell imaging, reagents that would generally increase dye permeability were not used. For this reason, the red-channel of Fig. 8(b), which was treated with R-CDs after spheroid formation, has a lower resolution than that of Fig. 8(a).

This study presents a microfluidic platform and associated cell culture protocols to facilitate the nanotoxicity screening of R-CDs to cancer cell multicellular spheroids derived from the U87 cell line. The R-CDs were prepared from the simple hydrothermal synthesis of p-phenylenediamine and thiourea in water. The resultant R-CDs have bright red emissions with a QY of 13%. Furthermore, these red CDs show good water dispersibility and biocompatibility, making them suitable for cellular imaging. We used R-CDs in two manners for spheroid staining, using them simultaneously with cell seeding and or using them after the spheroid formation. In both cases, spheroids were stained with R-CDs. It should be noted that a microwell-based microfluidic chip was used to produce the spheroids. The geometry of this chip is fully optimized for the production of single-sized spheroids and for conducting a nanotoxicity assay to them. Since the chip structure is three-dimensional, a high-resolution lab-made optical 3D printer was used to print the mold for PDMS casting. Finally, this study introduces biocompatible red fluorescent CDs suitable for live 3D cell imaging as well as a microfluidic device for performing the nanotoxicity assay on the 3D cell culture model.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to those who supported them, especially the Iranian Photonics, Laser, Advanced Materials, and Manufacturing Technologies Development Headquarters.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Introduction: Fluorescent Materials for Cell Imaging, Fluorescent Materials for Cell Imaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optimizing the synthesis of red-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots for use in bioimaging
ACS Appl. Nano Mater.

Mater. Today

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A red emissive two-photon fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for intracellular pH detection
Small

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New insight into the concept of carbonization degree in synthesis of carbon dots to achieve facile smartphone based sensing platform
Sci. Rep.
